text.title: graph-rag-production Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for graph-rag-production. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: When using LlamaIndex KnowledgeGraphIndex, set max_triplets_per_chunk to at least 10 and verify extraction coverage on a sample of dense chunks. LlamaIndex is tightly coupled to the OpenAI API format — migrating to Anthropic requires a custom LLM adapter; do not assume provider portability.
rule.rationale: The default max_triplets_per_chunk silently truncates dense academic or legal text at chunk extraction time, producing an incomplete graph without any error. OpenAI API coupling is not documented prominently; discovering it after a large index has been built on OpenAI keys and then switching providers is expensive.

rule.statement: When running Microsoft graphrag CLI in automated pipelines: checkpoint the generated config after the first run, pin the graphrag version, and track indexing cost by counting chunks before running. Do not run graphrag index without a cost estimate when the corpus exceeds 10,000 chunks.
rule.rationale: graphrag CLI rewrites config on first run and the config format changes between minor versions. A version bump mid-pipeline can force a full re-index. At $0.004/chunk (gpt-4o extraction), 100k chunks = $400 per index run; cost visibility before execution is mandatory.

rule.statement: For single-process Graph RAG pipelines with no concurrent access requirement, prefer Kuzu (embedded, zero server cost) over Neo4j. For multi-process or multi-user deployments, use Neo4j self-hosted (Docker) or Neo4j AuraDB. Do not use Amazon Neptune unless already committed to the AWS ecosystem — cost is 5-10x Neo4j AuraDB for equivalent workloads.
rule.rationale: Kuzu eliminates the operational overhead of running and maintaining a graph database server for workloads that do not need concurrent access. For most RAG indexing pipelines that index once and read from a single query process, the embedded pattern is the simplest and cheapest path.
